design_philosophy.core_loop: Economic micromanagement and resource production feeding military unit generation to conquer territories. Players build settlements with functioning economies, construct buildings, manage supply/demand chains, then leverage economic output into military conquest.
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Balance between economic development (building infrastructure, managing workers, producing resources) and military expansion (generating units, conquering rivals). Players must decide when to prioritize settlement growth versus aggressive expansion.
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Command a shipwrecked Roman expedition rebuilding civilization on an uncharted island, progressing through magical portals to encounter exotic civilizations (Nubians, Vikings, Japanese) while expanding territorial control.
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Strict supply-and-demand economy system where all unit generation and building requires careful resource chains—no direct unit purchase. This forces players into deep economic planning rather than pure tactical micromanagement. The game privileges economic literacy over real-time reflexes, distinguishing it from twitch-based RTS competitors.

difficulty.checkpointing: Mission-based progression in campaign; players must complete individual map objectives to advance. Free Game mode allows scenario customization by player.
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency in Free Game mode where players set custom rules and opponent count (2-4 races selectable). Campaign mode has fixed difficulty progression across ten missions, with additional nine missions in Mission CD expansion. Players cannot adjust difficulty mid-scenario but can restart missions.

progression.upgrade_system: Technology and building unlocks tied to economic development. As settlements produce resources and workers advance in skill, new building types and military unit types become available. No explicit leveling; progression is implicit through economic capacity.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Buildings and unit types unlock through prerequisite structures and resource availability. Campaign missions unlock sequentially; each mission completion grants access to next campaign chapter. Mission CD provides parallel campaign chain.
replayability_hooks: Free Game mode with customizable map rules, race selection, and opponent configuration
Multiple race campaigns (Romans in both base and Mission CD campaigns; Octavius's great-grandson conquest campaign adds world conquest layer)
Map editor allowing custom scenario creation
Multiplayer support against computer opponents and human players

world_building_techniques: Campaign framing narrative: Romans shipwrecked on uncharted island, led by Captain Octavius, seeking magical portals to return home
Encounter-based storytelling: progressive conflicts with distinct civilizations (Nubians, Vikings, Japanese) across campaign missions, each representing different aesthetic and cultural presence
Sequel narrative hook: Mission CD campaign features Octavius's great-grandson attempting world conquest, extending the generational saga
Four aesthetically distinct playable races with different visual identities (Romans, Nubians, Vikings, Japanese)

faction_indicators: Four distinct civilizations: Romans (player faction), Nubians, Vikings, Japanese—each with different architectural and cultural aesthetics
Campaign progression encounters different factions sequentially, creating implicit 'enemy faction' hierarchy
danger_signals: Military unit production and massing by opponent AI
Territorial expansion pressure from competing factions
Resource scarcity forcing economic choices between growth and defense
environmental_storytelling: Distinct architectural styles for each race visible in settlement construction
Island geography and portal locations frame exploration and territorial discovery
Economic infrastructure (production buildings, worker movement) visible on map reflects civilization development
